title: Inventory Systems That Scale: From Chaos to Control
subtitle: Running out of oat milk at 9am on a Saturday is a systems failure, not bad luck.

sections.heading: The Cost of Running Out
sections.body:
Running out of a key item during service is one of the most expensive failures in a cafe — and one of the most preventable.

When you run out of oat milk on a Saturday morning:
- Every oat-milk customer is disappointed (and oat-milk customers are often your most loyal specialty coffee drinkers)
- Some will switch to dairy. Some will leave. Some will not come back.
- The barista has to have an awkward conversation 30 times: "Sorry, we're out of oat milk today"
- Your Google reviews may gain a one-star mention

The cost is not the €3.50 carton you should have ordered. It is the €600/year regular who decides your cafe is unreliable.

Reactive ordering — waiting until something runs out and then scrambling — is the root cause. The fix is a system: par levels, regular counts, and an ordering rhythm tied to actual consumption.

sections.heading: Par Levels: The Foundation
sections.body:
A par level is the minimum quantity of an item you need to have on hand at any point. When stock drops to the par level, you reorder.

**How to calculate par levels:**

1. Track daily usage for 2 weeks
2. Calculate average daily usage
3. Multiply by lead time (days between ordering and receiving)
4. Add a safety buffer (20–30%)

**Example: Whole milk**
- Average daily usage: 15 litres
- Delivery lead time: 2 days
- Usage during lead time: 30 litres
- Safety buffer (25%): 7.5 litres
- **Par level: 37.5 litres (round to 38)**

When your stock hits 38 litres, order. This ensures you never run out, even if usage spikes slightly or delivery is delayed.

**Set par levels for everything that matters:**

| Category | Key Items |
|----------|-----------|
| Coffee | Beans (house, decaf, guest), filters |
| Dairy | Whole milk, semi-skimmed, oat, soy, almond |
| Food | Bread, key fillings, salad components |
| Bakery | Pastries (by type), cakes |
| Consumables | Cups (each size), lids, napkins, bags |
| Cleaning | Detergent, sanitiser, cloths |

Write par levels on a card in the stock room. When the closer does the end-of-day count, they check against par levels and add anything below par to the order list.

sections.heading: FIFO and Stock Rotation
sections.body:
FIFO — First In, First Out — is the principle that older stock gets used before newer stock. It sounds obvious. It is routinely ignored.

Use T116-01 (FIFO Rotation Guide):

**The problem FIFO solves:**
New delivery arrives. Staff stack new items on top of old ones. The old items get pushed to the back. They expire. They become waste. You paid for them twice — once to buy them, again to throw them away.

**FIFO in practice:**
1. When a delivery arrives, move existing stock to the front
2. Place new stock behind existing stock
3. Label everything with the date opened or the delivery date
4. Train every team member: take from the front, stock from the back

**Date labelling protocol:**
- Every item opened gets a date label (day dots or marker pen)
- Use-by dates face forward on shelves
- Freezer items get a freeze date and use-by label
- Prepped food gets a prep date and discard date

**Fridge organisation:**
- Top shelf: ready-to-eat items (least likely to drip)
- Middle shelves: dairy, eggs, prepped items
- Bottom shelf: raw meat and fish (if applicable) — lowest position to prevent drip contamination
- Door: nothing temperature-sensitive (door temperature fluctuates)
- Front: items expiring soonest

**The daily check:**
During opening, scan the fridge for anything approaching its use-by date. Items expiring today should be prioritised for sale or use. Items past their date are discarded immediately — no exceptions, no debates.

sections.heading: The Weekly Stock Count
sections.body:
Use T115-01 (Inventory Count Sheet) to perform a full count weekly.

**When:** Same day, same time each week. Most cafes count Sunday evening or Monday morning — after the weekend rush and before the Monday delivery.

**Who:** One person counts, one person records (or the same person if staff is limited). The counter should not be the person who placed the orders — separation reduces error and prevents concealment of losses.

**What to count:**
- Coffee beans (weigh partial bags)
- Milk (count full units, estimate open containers)
- Bakery/pastry items
- Key food ingredients (by weight or count)
- Packaging (cups, lids, bags — by sleeve or box)
- Cleaning supplies

**You do NOT need to count:**
- Condiments (salt, pepper, sugar) — track by order frequency, not by count
- Items with very low value and stable usage
- Anything you only order monthly

**What to do with the count:**

1. Compare to theoretical usage (what you should have used based on POS sales)
2. Calculate variance: (Opening stock + Purchases - Closing stock) vs. Theoretical usage
3. Investigate variances above 5%

Common causes of variance:
- **Positive variance** (less stock used than expected): Under-portioning, sales recorded incorrectly, items sold but not rung up under correct product
- **Negative variance** (more stock used than expected): Over-portioning, waste not logged, theft, stock received but not checked

The count itself takes 30–45 minutes for a typical cafe. The value it delivers — in waste prevention, cost control, and ordering accuracy — is worth hours of saved costs.

sections.heading: Ordering Rhythm
sections.body:
Good inventory management is not about counting. It is about ordering the right amount at the right time.

**Build an ordering schedule:**

| Day | Supplier | Items | Lead Time |
|-----|----------|-------|----------|
| Monday | Coffee roaster | Beans | 2 days (arrives Wed) |
| Monday | Dairy supplier | Milk, cream, butter | Next day (arrives Tue) |
| Tuesday | Bakery | Pastries, bread | Next day (arrives Wed) |
| Wednesday | Packaging supplier | Cups, lids, bags | 3 days (arrives Sat) |
| Thursday | Food supplier | Meat, produce, dry goods | Next day (arrives Fri) |

**Ordering process:**
1. Check current stock against par levels
2. Check upcoming events or unusual demand (holiday weekend, local event)
3. Calculate order quantity: Par level - Current stock + Expected above-normal demand
4. Place order before the supplier's cut-off time
5. Record the order (what was ordered, quantity, expected delivery date)

**Delivery receiving:**
- Check every delivery against the order: correct items, correct quantities, correct quality
- Check temperatures of chilled and frozen items
- Check use-by dates (reject anything too close to expiry)
- Sign the delivery note only after checking
- Report any issues to the supplier immediately
- Put stock away promptly using FIFO rotation

Never let a delivery sit unchecked. A box of chicken left on the counter for an hour is a food safety violation and a quality issue.

sections.heading: Inventory Excellence
sections.body:
Use T117-01 (Loss Prevention Checklist) to move from basic inventory management to excellence.

**Loss prevention:**

Most inventory loss in cafes is not theft. It is waste, spillage, over-portioning, and poor receiving. But theft does happen, and a good system makes it difficult:

- **Camera coverage** of stock areas (deterrent effect alone is significant)
- **Dual sign-off** on deliveries (receiving and checking are done by two people, or one person with a check by the manager)
- **Restricted access** to high-value stock (coffee beans, alcohol if applicable)
- **Staff meals tracked** (free staff meals are a benefit — untracked ones are a leak)
- **Void and discount reports** reviewed weekly (patterns of excessive voids from one staff member warrant investigation)

**Inventory KPIs:**

| KPI | Target | Red Flag |
|-----|--------|---------|
| Stock-outs per week | 0 | >2 |
| Inventory variance (%) | <2% | >5% |
| Days of stock on hand (avg) | 3–5 days | >10 days |
| Waste as % of purchases | <3% | >5% |
| Order accuracy (supplier) | 98%+ | <95% |

**Advanced: Inventory turnover.**
How quickly you use and replace stock. High turnover = fresh stock, low waste, efficient cash use. Low turnover = old stock sitting on shelves, cash tied up, higher waste risk.

Inventory turnover = Cost of goods sold ÷ Average inventory value

A healthy cafe turns inventory 50–100 times per year (weekly stock lasting about a week). If your turnover is below 30, you are over-ordering.
